title: श्री गुरु वन्दना
devanagariText:
॥ श्री गुरु वन्दना ॥

ॐ श्री गुरवे नमः। ॐ दत्तात्रेयाय नमः॥

गुरुर्ब्रह्मा गुरुर्विष्णुर्गुरुर्देवो महेश्वरः।
गुरुः साक्षात्परं ब्रह्म तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥ १॥

अखण्डमण्डलाकारं व्याप्तं येन चराचरम्।
तत्पदं दर्शितं येन तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥ २॥

अज्ञानतिमिरान्धस्य ज्ञानाञ्जनशलाकया।
चक्षुरुन्मीलितं येन तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥ ३॥

स्थावरं जंगमं व्याप्तं यत्किञ्चित्सचराचरम्।
तत्पदं दर्शितं येन तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥ ४॥

चिन्मयं व्यापि यत्सर्वं त्रैलोक्यं सचराचरम्।
तत्पदं दर्शितं येन तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥ ५॥

त्वमेव माता च पिता त्वमेव
त्वमेव बन्धुश्च सखा त्वमेव।
त्वमेव विद्या द्रविणं त्वमेव
त्वमेव सर्वं मम देवदेव॥ ६॥

ब्रह्मानन्दं परमसुखदं केवलं ज्ञानमूर्तिं
द्वन्द्वातीतं गगनसदृशं तत्त्वमस्यादिलक्ष्यम्।
एकं नित्यं विमलमचलं सर्वधीसाक्षिभूतं
भावातीतं त्रिगुणरहितं सद्गुरुं तं नमामि॥ ७॥

ध्यानमूलं गुरोर्मूर्तिः पूजामूलं गुरोः पदम्।
मन्त्रमूलं गुरोर्वाक्यं मोक्षमूलं गुरोः कृपा॥ ८॥

न गुरोरधिकं तत्त्वं न गुरोरधिकं तपः।
तत्त्वज्ञानात्परं नास्ति तस्मै श्रीगुरवे नमः॥ ९॥

गुरुपादाम्बुजं स्मृत्वा सर्वसिद्धिर्भवेद् ध्रुवम्।
दत्तात्रेयं जगद्गुरुं प्रणमामि मुहुर्मुहुः॥ १०॥

गुकारस्त्वन्धकारस्तु रुकारस्तन्निरोधकः।
अन्धकारनिरोधित्वात् गुरुरित्यभिधीयते॥ ११॥

गुरुवन्दनमिदं पुण्यं यः पठेद् भक्तिसंयुतः।
गुरुकृपां समाप्नोति ज्ञानमोक्षं च विन्दति॥ १२॥

॥ इति श्री गुरु वन्दना सम्पूर्णा ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Guru Vandana ||

Om Shri Gurave namah | Om Dattatreyaya namah ||

Gurur-Brahma Gurur-Vishnur-Gurur-Devo Maheshwarah |
Guruh sakshat Param Brahma tasmai Shri Gurave namah || 1 ||

Akhanda-mandalakaram vyaptam yena characharam |
Tat-padam darshitam yena tasmai Shri Gurave namah || 2 ||

Ajnana-timira-andhasya jnananjana-shalakaya |
Chakshur-unmilitam yena tasmai Shri Gurave namah || 3 ||

Sthavaram jangamam vyaptam yat-kinchit-sacharacharam |
Tat-padam darshitam yena tasmai Shri Gurave namah || 4 ||

Chinmayam vyapi yat-sarvam trailokyam sacharacharam |
Tat-padam darshitam yena tasmai Shri Gurave namah || 5 ||

Tvameva Mata cha Pita tvameva
Tvameva Bandhush-cha Sakha tvameva |
Tvameva Vidya Dravinam tvameva
Tvameva Sarvam mama Deva-Deva || 6 ||

Brahma-anandam parama-sukhadam kevalam jnana-murtim
Dvandva-atitam gagana-sadrisham tattvamasy-adi-lakshyam |
Ekam nityam vimalam-achalam sarva-dhi-sakshi-bhutam
Bhava-atitam triguna-rahitam Sadgurum tam namami || 7 ||

Dhyana-mulam Guror-murtih puja-mulam Guroh padam |
Mantra-mulam Guror-vakyam moksha-mulam Guroh kripa || 8 ||

Na Guror-adhikam tattvam na Guror-adhikam tapah |
Tattva-jnanat-param nasti tasmai Shri Gurave namah || 9 ||

Guru-padambujam smritva sarva-siddhir-bhaved dhruvam |
Dattatreyam Jagadgurum pranamami muhur-muhuh || 10 ||

Gu-karas-tv-andhakaras-tu Ru-karas-tan-nirodhakah |
Andhakara-nirodhitvat Gurur-ity-abhidhiyate || 11 ||

Guru-vandanam-idam punyam yah pathed bhakti-samyutah |
Guru-kripam samapnoti jnana-moksham cha vindati || 12 ||

|| Iti Shri Guru Vandana Sampurna ||
hindiMeaning: गुरु ब्रह्मा हैं, गुरु विष्णु हैं, गुरु देव महेश्वर हैं; गुरु साक्षात् परब्रह्म हैं — उन श्री गुरु को नमस्कार। अखण्ड मण्डलाकार, चराचर में व्याप्त — उस तत्त्व को जिसने दिखाया, उन गुरु को नमस्कार। अज्ञान-अन्धकार से अन्धे की आँखें जिसने ज्ञान-अंजन से खोलीं, उन गुरु को नमस्कार। आप ही माता हैं, पिता हैं, बन्धु हैं, सखा हैं, विद्या हैं, धन हैं, सर्वस्व हैं। ब्रह्मानन्द, परम सुखद, केवल ज्ञानमूर्ति, द्वन्द्वातीत, गगन-सदृश, तत्त्वमसि के लक्ष्य, एक, नित्य, विमल, अचल, सर्व बुद्धि के साक्षी, त्रिगुणरहित सद्गुरु को नमस्कार। ध्यान का मूल गुरु की मूर्ति, पूजा का मूल गुरु के चरण, मन्त्र का मूल गुरु का वाक्य, मोक्ष का मूल गुरु की कृपा है। 'गु' अन्धकार है और 'रु' उसका निरोध — अन्धकार मिटाने वाले को गुरु कहते हैं।
benefits: Invokes the grace of the Guru, the supreme teacher
Opens the inner eye of spiritual knowledge
Traditionally recited for removal of the darkness of ignorance
Invokes the blessing of all spiritual attainments (siddhis)
description: Dedicated to Lord Dattatreya, the Guru Vandana (श्री गुरु वन्दना) is a sacred prayer revered in Hindu tradition. Rooted in Guru Gita and Traditional Guru Stuti, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Devotees recite this prayer to bestow all spiritual attainments (siddhis) and establish the deepest Guru-disciple connection. It is also said to remove the darkness of ignorance. This sacred text is ideally recited on Thursday (Guruvar) and during Guru Purnima for enhanced blessings. Comprising 12 verses, it can be completed in approximately 8 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Guru Vandana in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Guru Vandana?
seoFaqs.answer: The Guru Vandana (श्री गुरु वन्दना) is a twelve-verse compilation of the most beloved guru-stuti shlokas in the Hindu tradition, anchored by the universally famous Guru Brahma Guru Vishnu, Guru Devo Maheshwarah verse and the Tvameva Mata cha Pita Tvameva shloka. The collection is drawn from the Guru Gita (Skanda Purana, Uttara Khanda — taught by Lord Shiva to Mata Parvati) and traditional Guru Stuti corpus, and includes (1) the Guru-Trimurti shloka (Guru as Brahma-Vishnu-Mahesh and Parabrahma); (2) Akhanda-mandalakaram — the cosmic guru pervading all; (3) Ajnana-timirandhasya — the guru as opener of the inner eye through jnana-anjana; (4) Tvameva Mata cha Pita — the guru as mother, father, friend, knowledge and wealth; (5) the Brahma-anandam supreme contemplation verse; (6) Dhyana-mulam Guror-murtih — the four-fold mula teaching; (7) Na Guror-adhikam tattvam — no truth higher than the guru; (8) the Gukara-Rukara etymology; (9) the closing phala-shruti. (Source: Guru Gita; Skanda Purana, Uttara Khanda.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Guru Brahma Guru Vishnu Guru Devo Maheshwarah?
seoFaqs.answer: This celebrated verse — the most universally known of all Hindu shlokas — declares the absolute identity of the Guru with the supreme Brahman: Gurur-Brahma — the Guru is Brahma (the creator of the disciple's spiritual life); Gurur-Vishnuh — the Guru is Vishnu (the sustainer of the disciple's sadhana); Guruh Devo Maheshwarah — the Guru is the Lord Maheshwara (Shiva, the destroyer of the disciple's ignorance and ego); Guruh Sakshat Param Brahma — the Guru is directly (sakshat) the Supreme Brahman itself; Tasmai Shri Gurave Namah — to that Sri Guru I prostrate. The verse is the foundation of the Hindu doctrine of Guru-tattva — that the externally-appearing human guru is, in essence, the supreme cosmic principle itself, and the disciple's salutation is therefore offered not to a person but to the eternal Brahman manifesting through that form. The verse is recited at the start of every Hindu ritual, every Vedanta class and every dance / music performance. (Source: Guru Gita; Skanda Purana.)

seoFaqs.question: What does Tvameva Mata Cha Pita Tvameva mean?
seoFaqs.answer: The verse Tvameva Mata cha Pita Tvameva, Tvameva Bandhush-cha Sakha Tvameva, Tvameva Vidya Dravinam Tvameva, Tvameva Sarvam Mama Deva-Deva is the supreme expression of total surrender (sharanagati) to the Guru / Ishvara. Word-by-word: Tvameva — You alone (are); Mata cha Pita — mother and father; Tvameva Bandhus-cha Sakha — You alone are kinsman and friend; Tvameva Vidya Dravinam — You alone are knowledge and wealth; Tvameva Sarvam Mama Deva-Deva — You alone are everything to me, O God of gods. The verse is universally recited as the closing benediction of every Hindu prayer, every aarti, every kirtana session, and is the most beloved samarpana (offering-up) verse in the entire devotional corpus. It expresses the bhakta's recognition that all relationships, all knowledge, all wealth, all support — every category of refuge — is fulfilled and transcended in the one supreme Lord-Guru. (Source: traditional sharanagati / samarpana verse, attached to Guru Stuti corpus.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the etymology Gukaras-tv-andhakaras-tu Rukaras-tan-nirodhakah teach?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 11 of the Guru Vandana gives the classical Tantric etymology of the word Guru: Gu-karas-tv-andhakaras-tu — the syllable Gu means darkness (andhakara, ignorance, avidya); Ru-karas-tan-nirodhakah — the syllable Ru means the dispeller (nirodhaka) of that darkness; Andhakara-nirodhitvat Gurur-ity-abhidhiyate — therefore one who dispels the darkness of ignorance is called Guru. This nirukta (etymological derivation) appears in the Advayataraka Upanishad, the Guru Gita and the Vishvasara Tantra, and is the foundational definition of guru-tattva in the Hindu tradition. It establishes that the title Guru is not a social or hereditary designation but an absolute spiritual qualification — only one who can actually dispel the disciple's avidya and reveal the Self deserves the name. The very utterance of the syllables Gu-Ru therefore carries the cosmic function of darkness-removal. (Sources: Advayataraka Upanishad; Guru Gita; Vishvasara Tantra.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Guru Vandana be recited for maximum benefit?
seoFaqs.answer: The Guru Vandana is most powerfully recited on Guru Purnima (Ashadha Shukla Purnima — the supreme annual festival of the guru-shishya tradition, the day Veda Vyasa is worshipped as Adi-Guru), Thursday (Guruvar — the weekly day of the Guru / Brihaspati), Datta Jayanti (since Dattatreya is the Adi-Guru of the Avadhuta tradition), Shankara Jayanti, Ramana Maharshi Jayanti, and on the personal jayanti / aradhana day of one's own diksha-guru. The phala-shruti specifies daily recitation as part of every sadhaka's morning practice — before any other puja, japa or stotra-paatha — to invoke the guru's blessing on the day's spiritual effort. It is also recited at the start of every Vedanta study session, every classical music / dance practice (where the guru-tattva is invoked first), every Bharatanatyam / Kathak performance, and at every Indian wedding when the bride and groom touch the elders' feet. Sannyasis recite it daily. (Source: classical guru-shishya parampara tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Guru Vandana grant according to tradition?
seoFaqs.answer: The phala-shruti (Guru-vandanam-idam punyam yah pathed bhakti-samyutah, Guru-kripam samapnoti jnana-moksham cha vindati) promises that one who recites this Guru-vandana with devotion attains (1) Guru-kripa — the supreme grace of the Guru, the indispensable foundation of all spiritual progress; and (2) Jnana-moksha — knowledge of the Self and final liberation. Tradition expands this into specific blessings: (3) destruction of the inner darkness of avidya (verse 3 — Ajnana-timira-andhasya); (4) opening of the inner eye of jnana through the guru's collyrium-stick (jnana-anjana-shalaka); (5) revelation of the supreme Atma-tattva pervading all moving and unmoving creation; (6) attainment of all spiritual siddhis (Guru-padambujam smritva sarva-siddhir-bhaved dhruvam, verse 10); (7) the supreme realisation that all categories — mother, father, friend, knowledge, wealth — converge in the one Guru-Brahman; and (8) admission into the guru-shishya parampara of the lineage. (Source: Guru Vandana phala-shruti.)
